PIECES OF HISTORY This and That TOPIC 120

* The book “The Craft of Nombrynge” – issued in the year 1300 said: “Here tells that there ben 7 spices or partes to the crafte. The first is Addicion, the secund is called Subtracion.The thyrd is called Duplacion; the 4 is Dimydicion; the 5 is called Multiplication. The 6 is called Diuision. The 7 is called Extraction of the Rote”.

Solve for x in each case:

a. x = 7 + 4 b. x + 8 = 13 c. x = 45.(3) d. 5x = 35 e. 2435\sqrt[5]{243}5243​ f. x2 =36.x^{2}\ = 36.x2 =36.

The Meaning Of the 6 Main Operations Between Numbers:

Addition is Repeated Counting. 7 + 4 “Eight”, “Nine”, “Ten”, “Eleven”.

Subtraction is Repeated Un-counting. 12 – 7; ’11’, ’10’, ’9’, ’8’, ’7’ ,’6’, ’FIVE’.

Multiplication is Repeated Addition. 45 + 45 + 45 = 3(40) + 3(5) =120 + 15 = 135.

Division is Repeated Subtraction. 35 – 5 – 5 – 5 – 5 – 5 – 5 – 5 = 0. 7 times!

Powers is Repeated Multiplication.  35 =3 . 3 . 3 . 3 . 3\ 3^{5}\ = 3\ .\ 3\ .\ 3\ .\ 3\ .\ 3 35 =3 . 3 . 3 . 3 . 3 = 243.

Root is Repeated Un-powering, Repeated Division. 36 ÷2 ÷3 ÷2 ÷3;\div 2\ \div 3\ \div 2\ \div 3;÷2 ÷3 ÷2 ÷3; 36 = 6 x 6

*Edward Waring (1784 to 1793) said “Any odd number can be written as the sum of 3 Prime Numbers”. Sadly, he said that 1 is a Prime Number. How ‘correct’ is he?

*Christian Goldbach (1690 to 1764) wrote to Euler that “Any even number can be written as the sum of 2 primes plus 1”. He also thought 1 was prime, so later had to say ‘Any even number greater than 5’. Even with the Computer Age, going up to 400 billion billion, he has not been proven wrong – yet this has not been rigidly proven. So it remains a conjecture. The “Incompleteness of Mathematics”.

*Edward Kasner ( 1878 to 1955) asked his 9 year old nephew Milton Sirotto to give a name to the huge number 1010010^{100}10100. Sir Arthur Edlington (died 1944) had said that there were 108910^{89}1089 particles in the entire universe. Kasner said that we therefore did not need a number bigger than 1010010^{100}10100. The young Milton called it a GOOGOL. Later some guy said what of 10googol10^{googol}10googol called a Googleplex. That is exactly what the nephew said.

*Diophantes spoke of a man who enjoyed his youth for 16\frac{1}{6}61​ of his life; grew a beard for 112\frac{1}{12}121​ of his life, then after 17\frac{1}{7}71​ of his life he married. 5 years later he had a son, who lived for 12\frac{1}{2}21​ of his father’s life. The man died 4 years later. How old was he when he died?

g>*Muhammad ibn Musa al-Khwarizmi, a Persian, wrote a thesis in 830 AD on Al – gabr, called Kitāb al-muḫtaṣar fī ḥisāb Al-ğabr wa-l-muqābala.

For this he earns the title ‘Father of Algebra’, as was Diophantes so regarded. Image 150

With the recent interest in Fibonacci, (Leonardo of Pisa) – son of Bonaccio, certain commentators refer to him as ‘great’ He was a fine trader whose father operated from Algeria where Leonardo learnt Algebra from Moslem tutors. He loved their numerals and wrote the book Liber Abaci (1202) to popularise the Arab-Indian numbers to the benefit of Traders. The series appears in his book with no comment.

* Circa 626 AD Brahmagupta denied that (-) . (-) = +

You accept that ‘Minus times minus equals plus’.

But why? Can you verify this?

He clearly explained zero, not just as a place holder, but as ‘nothing’. He mastered the solving of equations, and was the first to state the Sign laws. An astronomer, he wrote many books on Mathematics. Image 151

In 1202 Fibonacci sensed that numbers could be negative, as being the son of a wealthy trader, he said “A negative is a loss”.

In 1603 Simon Stevin, who wrote one of the first books on the decimal system said that ‘minus’ was not evil. In those days in Holland, the rigorous religious leaders would not accept ‘negative’ numbers as God would not create ‘evil’.

The Ars Magma of Gerolano Cardono was a known book on Mathematics in 1545. He regarded Negative numbers as ‘frivilous’.

Rene Descartes, the great philosopher whose thinking stifled Europe - creating the dichotomy of body and soul - said in 1637 that negative numbers were ‘false roots’. He was the first to use alphabetic letters to represent numbers: x as the unknown.

In 1685, John Wallis said that if 5 > 4, then 15 <  14\frac{1}{5}\ < \ \ \frac{1}{4}51​ <  41​. But then he said that 1 > 0 and got stuck. Why? A great Mathematician who was the first to use the symbol ∞\infty∞ for infinity. He was a specialist in Cryptography. He said of himself as a schoolboy: “It was always my affection, even from a child, not only to learn by rote, but to know the reasons of what I learnt; to inform my judgement as well as to furnish my memory”. He could have said: “Give Meaning To Maths!”.

an style="color: #ff0000">an style="color: #ff0000">m>* The great friendship: G.H. Hardy (1877 to 1947) and Srinivasa Ramanujan (1887 to 1920). G.H.Hardy was a brilliant - but very shy (he covered all mirrors in his house) - Pure Mathematician at Cambridge University. In 1913 he finally decided to meet the phenomenal Indian, who was basically unschooled, but through his religion had said that his thoughts were divinely inspired, and that he received them in dreams.

"An equation for me has no meaning”, Ramanujan once said, "unless it expresses a thought of God_in_Hinduism">God". He created a formula for π\piπ that has been developed by others to now go to 10 trillion places and still not terminating nor recurring.

It was tough for Ramanuyan to get accepted at Cambridge, because although he came out with wonderful and accurate ideas, he did not know how to formally prove them. He was very sickly but Hardy befriended him, heard his ideas and taught him Mathematical Method. Many modern thinkers are pursuing his ‘methods’.

Hardy: “Beauty is the first test: there is no permanent place in the world for ugly Mathematics”.

“A mathematician, like a painter or a poet, is a maker of patterns. If his patterns are more permanent than theirs, it is because they are made with ideas”.

Image 252 Image 252

Whether it was a taxi, or Hardy’s car, the story goes that the London cab driver, having heard Ramanujan talk on Numbers on radio, said to him:

“I bet you can say nothing interesting about my cab number 1729.”

”On the contrary”, said Ramanujan.

“It is the smallest number that can be written as the sum of 2 cubes in 2 different ways” Like a3 +  b3a^{3}\ + \ \ b^{3}a3 +  b3 and c3 +  d3c^{3}\ + \ \ d^{3}c3 +  d3.

Challenge: What is the value of a, b, c, d?

See Solutions for these values and for some other unique patterns from 1729.

True Story:

Just before my elder daughter started school, we moved into a house numbered 22. She asked if we lived in Number 4. Tough getting out of that one. Less than a minute thereafter she asked is 16 + 16 = 32. She said that she kept on adding. A few months later, I was called in by the teacher who told me that she only got 1 out of 500 questions correct, and that they had never had such a weak pupil. I asked her to add 5 + 4. She said 8. Instead of “Six, Seven, Eight, Nine”, she said: “Five, Six, Seven, Eight”. One technical error but the correct concept - and there was consistency in the 499 errors, when checked. Children often think differently to the Teacher, who happens to love and propagate the dubious security of Rote. As the A.I.Age hits us, Rote Memorising will inhibit the chances of children.

The Abacus

Image 253 As Mathematics evolved in China, it supported great engineering projects, and became one of the finer Arts. Formal proofs were not expected. When dynasties were overthrown, often the intellectuals were killed and books and parchments were burned. Thus one had to learn off by heart. Each successive dynasty had its own focus, often reducing Mathematicians to mere calculators on the Abacus – or to memory. Gerbert of Auriliac (946 to 1003), later to be Pope Sylvester II – the first French pope - brought the Abacus, and the Hindu Decimal system to Europe.

SOLUTIONS PIECES OF HISTORY TOPIC 120

(i) x = 7 + 4 Seven; 8; 9; 10; 11 repeated counting.

(ii) x + 8 = 13 Thirteen; 12; 11; 10; 9; 8; 7; 6; 5 Repeated un-counting.

(iii) x = 45 (3) 45 + 45 + 45 = 135. Repeated Addition.

(iv) 5x = 35 5 parts of 7. Quotition.

or 35 – 5 = 30; 30 – 5 = 25; 25 – 5 = 20; 20 – 5 = 15;

15 – 5 = 10; 10 – 5 = 5 ; 5 – 5 = 0. Repeated Subtraction.

(v)    2435(v)\ \ \ \ \sqrt[5]{243}(v)    5243​ 243 = 3.3.3.3.3 Answer is 3. Repeated Division.

(vi)    x2 =36\ \ \ \ x^{2}\ = 36    x2 =36 6 x 6 = 36 (Repeated Multiplication) but -6 x -6 = 36

x2x^{2}x2 = 36 or x2x^{2}x2 = 36

                                x2\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ x^{2}                                x2 - 36 = 0 x = ± 36\pm \ \sqrt{36}± 36​

(x + 6) (x – 6) = 0 x = 6 or x = -6

x = -6 or x = 6

* For Waring to be proven wrong, you only need to find 1 example that does not fit what he says. 17 = 11 + 5 + 1, because he took 1 as a prime.

But 17 = 3 + 7 +7, but can a Prime be repeated? Yes, according to Waring.

* What if little Milton had to say googolgoogol{googol}^{googol}googolgoogol, and then you came along and said googolgoogol{googol}^{googol}googolgoogol + 1”.

Reverend John Wallis Edward Waring Edward Kasner

1616 - 1703 1773 - 1798 1878 - 1955

Image 254 Image 255 Image 256

We looked at this contradiction in the Topic on Spot The Error. Wallis acted as cryptographer for the British Parliament. In trying to verify that 1x\frac{1}{x}x1​ was greater than infinity, Wallis was the first to use the number line as we know it. He was a bad sleeper, and used to do mental calculations, like getting 53\sqrt{53}53​ to 27 places, and then tell someone the answer in the morning.

Diophantes : Solution: Let his age be x at death

1x6 +  1x12 +  1x7 +  51 +  1x2 +  41  \frac{\mathbf{1x}}{\mathbf{6}}\mathbf{\ + \ \ }\frac{\mathbf{1x}}{\mathbf{12}}\mathbf{\ + \ \ }\frac{\mathbf{1x}}{\mathbf{7}}\mathbf{\ + \ \ }\frac{\mathbf{5}}{\mathbf{1}}\mathbf{\ + \ \ }\frac{\mathbf{1x}}{\mathbf{2}}\mathbf{\ + \ \ }\frac{\mathbf{4}}{\mathbf{1}}\mathbf{\ \ }61x​ +  121x​ +  71x​ +  15​ +  21x​ +  14​   =   24x+7x+12x+168+42x+33684\mathbf{= \ \ \ }\frac{\mathbf{24x + 7x + 12x + 168 + 42x + 336}}{\mathbf{84}}=   8424x+7x+12x+168+42x+336​ = 85x+50484\mathbf{\ }\frac{\mathbf{85x + 504}}{\mathbf{84}} 8485x+504​

What is significant is the denominator of 84. He is unlikely to be a multiple of 84 (like 168) nor a factor of 84 (like 42). By insight or by guessing, the man was 84.

Solution: G.H. Hardy & Ramanuyan are so well remembered by the number 1729 that equals 13 +  123   and  103 +  93\mathbf{1}^{\mathbf{3}}\mathbf{\ + \ \ }\mathbf{12}^{\mathbf{3}}\mathbf{\ \ \ and\ \ }\mathbf{10}^{\mathbf{3}}\mathbf{\ + \ \ }\mathbf{9}^{\mathbf{3}}13 +  123   and  103 +  93.

1 + 7 + 2 + 9 = 19, and switching the digits we get 91.

91 = 43+ 33\mathbf{4}^{\mathbf{3}}\mathbf{+ \ }\mathbf{3}^{\mathbf{3}}43+ 33, and if negative numbers are allowed, then 63 +  (−5)3\mathbf{6}^{\mathbf{3}}\mathbf{\ + \ \ }\mathbf{( - 5)}^{\mathbf{3}}63 +  (−5)3


STEPHEN HAWKING (1942 to 2016) TOPIC 121

From the age of 21, after an active youth, Hawking suffered from motor neurone disease – correctly termed Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis - but this did not stop him investigating, and from succeeding in his studies – mainly to do with Cosmology.

From the Big Bang idea of George Le Maitre, he added Quantitative Gravitation to the known ideas of Space and Time and agreed that Black Holes were so dense that no gravity nor light could escape. He spoke of time moving backwards, and of 11 dimensions. The key concept was Gravity and its role in studying cosmology.

He studied his Cosmology – explanation of the Universe - at Oxford University, then to Cambridge, where he held Newton’s chair as Lucasion Professor of Mathematics.

Obviously his ideas will be questioned, but they have been justified by rigorous Mathematics. No Scientist can claim to be absolutely correct. Well, he should not, as he deals with Hypotheses – that is ‘Proposals’ or tenets of what is the least criticisible opinion at that moment in time. Many have taken his hypotheses as facts.

The scientist knows that someone will question his hypothesis and then proposes an alternate less criticible postulation. And thus ‘knowledge’ expands.

The Man The Book The Black Hole

Image 257 Image 258 Image 259

Quote from Hawking’s “A Brief History of Time”

“Even if there was only one possible unified theory (re construction of the universe) it is just a set of rules and equations. What is it that breathes fire into the equations and makes a universe for them (the scientists) to describe … Why does the universe go to the bother of existing?”

“However, if we do discover a complete theory, it should be understandable in broad principle by everyone, not just a few scientists. Then we shall all - philosophers, scientists, and just ordinary people - be able to take part in the discussion of the question of why it is that we and all the universe exist. If we find an answer to that, it would be the ultimate triumph of human reason – for then we will know the mind of God.”

From postulating the Black Hole concept in 1971, he considered – in 1981 - that the universe had no start and no end. Before him, La Maitre, in 1931 had spoken of the Big Bang as a starting point. Hubble had shown the Universe to be dynamic – new stars forming – new galaxies forming. Hawking was to refuse a Knighthood.

  • Is the Question more significant than the Answer? Question The Question.

Q20. 60 ) 1 0 0 0 0 0 0 seconds

= 1 6 6 6 6, minutes remainder 40 seconds

60 ) 1 6 6 6 6 minutes

= 2 7 7 hours remainder 46 minutes

24 ) 2 7 7 hours

= 1 1 days remainder 13 hours

11 days 13 hours 46 minutes and 40 seconds.

Yet when there are big sporting events the organisers will tell you that the 100000 tickets sold out in 10 minutes.

10000 seconds = 166 minutes 46seconds = 27 hours 40mn 46sec = 1 day 3 hours 40 minutes 46 seconds.

Could Sports’ Organisers really process the sale of 100000 tickets in 10 minutes?
